script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'glamdring-riposte-rampage-review',
  description: 'Adversarial multi-dimension review of the Riposte & Rampage implementation: GDScript/Godot correctness, gameplay logic/edge-cases, and docs consistency',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Review', detail: 'three independent reviewers (engine correctness, gameplay logic, docs consistency)' },
    { title: 'Verify', detail: 'adversarially re-check each non-trivial finding before it is reported' },
  ],
}

const REPO = 'C:\\Users\\duran\\Documents\\godot\\glamdring'

const CONTEXT = `
PROJECT: Glamdring — 1v1 reaction combat, Godot 4.6, fixed 16 ticks/sec. Player invincible (v1),
goal = enemy hp to 0. All gameplay logic in fight.gd _on_tick(). Tunables in balance.json via the
Balance autoload. Sprites = one cell per stance, region_rect swap, NO animation; new visual states
reuse cells + a modulate tint. No physics / no Area2D.

A feature package "RIPOSTE & RAMPAGE" was just implemented. Summary of intended behavior:
- F1 PERFECT PARRY -> STAGGER + RIPOSTE: active parry (player blocking a WEAK enemy swing within
  block_active_ticks of holding block) drains enemy fatigue AND arms a riposte (player.set_riposte_ready).
  While riposte_ready_ticks > 0, the player's next DAMAGING hit is x riposte_damage_multiplier, then
  consumed (a whiff does not consume). PERFECT parry (blocking_ticks_elapsed <= perfect_parry_ticks at
  swing resolve, a strict subset of active parry) additionally staggers the enemy: new "staggered"
  state, wide open, all hits land, for stagger_ticks.
- F2 HIT-STOP: fight.gd freezes the tick loop for hitstop_* real seconds on impacts (weak/strong/parry/
  stagger), while shake+particles+redraw keep running. Gameplay-neutral.
- F3 COMBO + RANK: player.combo increments on every damaging player hit; resets on getting hit or after
  combo_timeout_ticks; max_combo tracks best. Rank C/B/A/S by thresholds 1/5/10/15. HUD shows combo+rank
  top-center and max combo on win.
- F4 ENEMY FEINTS: a weak threat is, with feint_probability, a FEINT — telegraphs identically but does
  NOT swing; snaps to a short idle (feint_idle_ticks) and re-decides. Strong never feints.
- F5 READABILITY TINTS + EXECUTE: threat-onset pulse tint (threat_pulse_ticks), staggered cyan tint,
  player riposte-ready gold tint; execute = when enemy.hp <= execute_threshold_hp, player hits deal
  x execute_damage_multiplier (stacks with riposte; round once at end) and enemy shows a red tint.
  Modulate priority (highest first): hit_flash > (player riposte / enemy staggered) > threat pulse >
  execute > white.

TICK ORDER in fight._on_tick(): player.tick(); enemy.tick(); player.handle_input(); resolve enemy swing;
resolve player swing; fatigue->exhausted; win check; hud.

FILES (all under ${REPO}): fight.gd, player.gd, enemy.gd, enemy_basic.gd, combatant.gd, hud.gd,
balance.gd, balance.json, gameplay.md, plan.md, sprites/SPRITES.md, CLAUDE.md.
`

const FINDINGS_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['dimension', 'findings', 'overallAssessment'],
  properties: {
    dimension: { type: 'string' },
    overallAssessment: { type: 'string', description: 'one paragraph: is the implementation correct/shippable?' },
    findings: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        additionalProperties: false,
        required: ['severity', 'file', 'location', 'issue', 'suggestedFix', 'confidence'],
        properties: {
          severity: { type: 'string', enum: ['blocker', 'major', 'minor', 'nit'] },
          file: { type: 'string' },
          location: { type: 'string', description: 'function name / line range' },
          issue: { type: 'string', description: 'concrete description of the problem and why it is wrong' },
          suggestedFix: { type: 'string' },
          confidence: { type: 'string', enum: ['high', 'medium', 'low'] },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}

const VERDICT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['isReal', 'severity', 'reasoning', 'fix'],
  properties: {
    isReal: { type: 'boolean', description: 'true if this is a genuine defect that should be fixed' },
    severity: { type: 'string', enum: ['blocker', 'major', 'minor', 'nit', 'not-a-bug'] },
    reasoning: { type: 'string', description: 'after re-reading the actual code, why real or not' },
    fix: { type: 'string', description: 'concrete fix if real, else ""' },
  },
}

const DIMENSIONS = [
  {
    key: 'engine',
    brief: `ENGINE CORRECTNESS. Read fight.gd, player.gd, enemy.gd, enemy_basic.gd, combatant.gd, hud.gd, balance.gd.
Check for GDScript 4.x syntax/type errors, Godot 4.6 API misuse, runtime errors (null refs, wrong arg counts,
type mismatches int vs float, StringName vs String, Dictionary with conflicting keys, calling undefined symbols),
and CROSS-FILE CONTRACT MISMATCHES (does fight.gd call methods/fields that exist with the exact signatures in
player.gd/enemy.gd/hud.gd? does hud.update arg count match the call?). Verify _process hitstop pump cannot
deadlock or skip swing resolution, and that round()/int() conversions are valid. Flag anything that would fail
to parse or crash at runtime.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'gameplay',
    brief: `GAMEPLAY LOGIC & EDGE CASES. Read fight.gd, player.gd, enemy.gd, enemy_basic.gd, gameplay.md. Trace the
tick order. Verify: (1) riposte is armed only on active parry, consumed only on a damaging hit, never double-
applied; (2) perfect parry is a strict subset of active parry and correctly triggers stagger; (3) staggered enemy
takes hits and cannot act, and exits to idle; (4) feints never swing (incl. the defensive same-zero-tick branch),
don't soft-lock, and re-decide; (5) combo increments only on damage, resets on player hit + timeout, max_combo
persists; (6) execute & riposte multipliers stack and round once; (7) hit-stop is gameplay-neutral and does not
drop inputs in a broken way or desync tick_acc; (8) no new exploit (e.g., infinite stagger-lock, perfect-parry
trivializing the fight, feint infinite loop). Flag balance/design risks too, but mark them minor/nit unless they
break the game.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'docs',
    brief: `DOCS CONSISTENCY. Read gameplay.md, plan.md, sprites/SPRITES.md, balance.json, balance.gd, CLAUDE.md, and
the .gd files as needed. Verify the docs were actually updated to match the shipped code: every new balance.json
key appears in plan.md's JSON block + tunable tables; gameplay.md documents riposte/perfect-parry/staggered/feint/
execute/combo with values matching balance.json (riposte x1.6, perfect_parry 3t, stagger 16t, feint 0.15,
execute <=25hp x1.5, combo timeout 48t, rank tiers 1/5/10/15); FSM diagrams include staggered + feint; SPRITES.md
documents the staggered cell reuse + tints + modulate priority. Flag any doc that is missing, stale, or contradicts
the code (wrong number, missing state, etc). Also check CLAUDE.md doesn't need an update for the new mechanics.`,
  },
]

phase('Review')
const reviews = await pipeline(
  DIMENSIONS,
  d => agent(CONTEXT + '\n\nYOUR REVIEW DIMENSION:\n' + d.brief +
    '\n\nRead the actual files (do not trust this summary over the code). Report concrete, located findings with a suggested fix and your confidence. Be precise; an empty findings list is fine if the code is clean.',
    { label: 'review:' + d.key, phase: 'Review', schema: FINDINGS_SCHEMA, agentType: 'Explore' }),
  // Verify stage: adversarially re-check each non-nit finding against the real code.
  (review, dim) => {
    const toCheck = (review && review.findings ? review.findings : []).filter(f => f.severity !== 'nit')
    if (toCheck.length === 0) return review
    return parallel(toCheck.map(f => () =>
      agent('Adversarially verify this claimed defect by reading the ACTUAL code at ' + REPO + '. ' +
        'Default to isReal=false unless you can point to the exact code that is genuinely wrong. ' +
        'Claim:\nfile: ' + f.file + '\nlocation: ' + f.location + '\nseverity(claimed): ' + f.severity +
        '\nissue: ' + f.issue + '\nsuggestedFix: ' + f.suggestedFix +
        '\n\nContext for intended behavior:\n' + CONTEXT,
        { label: 'verify:' + dim.key + ':' + (f.file || '').split('\\').pop(), phase: 'Verify', schema: VERDICT_SCHEMA, agentType: 'Explore' })
        .then(v => ({ ...f, verdict: v }))
    )).then(verified => ({ dimension: review.dimension, overallAssessment: review.overallAssessment, findings: verified }))
  }
)

return reviews.filter(Boolean)

result.overallAssessment: The implementation is correct and shippable from an engine standpoint. All GDScript 4.x syntax is valid, Godot 4.6 APIs are used correctly, method signatures match across all files, type conversions (int/float, StringName/String) are properly handled, tick counting logic is sound, and the hitstop pump mechanism cannot deadlock. No runtime errors or contract mismatches detected.

The RIPOSTE & RAMPAGE feature implementation is largely correct with proper riposte arming/consumption mechanics, perfect parry stagger triggering, feint handling, combo tracking, damage multiplier stacking, and hit-stop freeze logic all functioning as intended. However, there is one significant gameplay logic bug: the active parry window duration is off by one tick (7 ticks instead of the specified 8 ticks). This affects the core parry mechanic and will cause players to lose the riposte benefit on blocks that extend into the 8th tick of their block state. The bug is easily fixed by changing one comparison operator. All other gameplay edge cases and exploit prevention check out cleanly.

findings.issue: Active parry window is 7 ticks instead of 8 ticks. The function checks `blocking_ticks_elapsed < Balance.block_active_ticks` where block_active_ticks = 8, but since blocking_ticks_elapsed increments on each tick starting from 1, the condition is true for values 1-7 (7 ticks total). The spec requires 'first 8 ticks' of blocking to be active parry. This means weak enemy attacks blocked on ticks 8+ of the player's block state will not drain fatigue or arm riposte, even though they should.
findings.suggestedFix: Change line 209 from `blocking_ticks_elapsed < Balance.block_active_ticks` to `blocking_ticks_elapsed <= Balance.block_active_ticks`. This makes the active window 8 ticks (elapsed values 1-8) matching the spec. The is_perfect_parry() check at line 237 already correctly uses `<=`, so this aligns the logic.

verdict.reasoning: The active parry window is currently 7 ticks instead of 8. On line 209, `is_active_block()` checks `blocking_ticks_elapsed < Balance.block_active_ticks` where `block_active_ticks = 8`. Since `blocking_ticks_elapsed` starts at 0 and increments to 1 on the first tick (line 121), the condition is true for values 1-7 only (7 ticks total). The spec requires the first 8 ticks to be active parry. This means a weak enemy swing blocked on tick 8 will not trigger active parry effects (fatigue drain, riposte arm). The related function `is_perfect_parry()` at line 237 already correctly uses `<=` with `perfect_parry_ticks`, confirming that `<=` is the intended comparison operator. Weak attacks blocked after tick 7 will bypass active parry logic entirely, preventing enemy fatigue drain and riposte arming on those ticks.
verdict.fix: Change line 209 from `return state == &"blocking" and blocking_ticks_elapsed < Balance.block_active_ticks` to `return state == &"blocking" and blocking_ticks_elapsed <= Balance.block_active_ticks`. This makes the active window 8 ticks (elapsed values 1-8) matching the spec and the tunable. This also aligns the logic with `is_perfect_parry()` which correctly uses `<=`.

result.overallAssessment: The documentation is substantially out of sync with the implemented code and balance.json. Multiple core timing values (threat_strong, exhausted, idle ranges, strong_probability) are documented with incorrect hardcoded numbers that contradict the actual balance.json file by 1–20 ticks. While the code correctly reads from balance.json (the true source), the narrative documentation in gameplay.md and plan.md will mislead readers. The FSM diagrams and tunable tables must be updated to reflect the actual balance.json values. Additionally, enemy.gd's base-class default for _stagger_ticks() hardcodes 12 instead of the canonical 16. This is shippable from a gameplay perspective (enemy_basic correctly uses Balance.stagger_ticks = 16), but the docs must be synchronized before the next review cycle.

findings.issue: threat_strong_ticks documented as 16, but balance.json (actual) has 12. This causes a 4-tick discrepancy in enemy strong attack telegraph duration.

verdict.reasoning: Direct file comparison confirms a genuine discrepancy. plan.md line 102 documents "threat_strong_ticks": 16, but balance.json line 11 shows the actual tuned value is 12. This is a 4-tick (0.25 second) difference in enemy strong attack telegraph duration. The defect is major because it creates false documentation that contradicts the actual game behavior, potentially misleading developers trying to understand or tune the game's attack timing. The threat_strong_ticks value directly affects gameplay readability and player reaction time windows during combat.
verdict.fix: Update plan.md line 102 from "threat_strong_ticks": 16, to "threat_strong_ticks": 12, to match the actual balance.json value.

findings.issue: exhausted_ticks documented as 64, but balance.json (actual) has 48. This shortens the exhausted state by 1 full second (16 ticks).

verdict.reasoning: The plan.md file at lines 102-107 (the JSON block under the "balance.json" section) documents that exhausted_ticks is 64, but the actual balance.json file at line 13 shows exhausted_ticks is 48. This is a genuine documentation-to-code mismatch of 16 ticks (1 full second at 16 ticks/sec). The plan.md explicitly states at line 89: "Tabulky níže slouží jako reference defaultů; reálné runtime hodnoty ber z JSONu" (Tables below serve as reference defaults; take real runtime values from the JSON). Since the JSON is the source of truth and the documented default in plan.md does not match it, this is a real defect that could mislead developers about the intended exhausted state duration.

findings.issue: idle_min_ticks documented as 32, but balance.json (actual) has 12. This causes minimum idle to be only 0.75 seconds instead of 2 seconds.

verdict.reasoning: The plan.md JSON block (lines 102-107) documents idle_min_ticks as 32 ticks, but balance.json (line 14) contains the actual value of 12 ticks. At 16 ticks/second, this is a 2.0-second vs 0.75-second discrepancy. The actual game runs with 12, making the minimum idle time 4x shorter than documented. This is a genuine documentation bug that would cause confusion for anyone tuning or understanding enemy behavior. Additionally, several other fields in the same block are also outdated (threat_strong_ticks: 16→12, exhausted_ticks: 64→48, idle_max_ticks: 64→48, strong_probability: 0.20→0.15).
verdict.fix:
Update plan.md lines 102-107 to match the actual balance.json values:
- Line 102: change threat_strong_ticks from 16 to 12
- Line 104: change exhausted_ticks from 64 to 48
- Line 105: change idle_min_ticks from 32 to 12 and idle_max_ticks from 64 to 48
- Line 106: change strong_probability from 0.20 to 0.15

findings.issue: idle_max_ticks documented as 64, but balance.json (actual) has 48. This reduces maximum idle by 1 full second (16 ticks).

verdict.reasoning: Adversarial re-read of actual code confirms the defect. plan.md lines 102-107 show a JSON block with outdated balance constants. The idle_max_ticks is documented as 64 in plan.md but the actual balance.json file (lines 10-17, the source of truth for runtime behavior) specifies 48. Additionally, several other constants in this JSON block are stale: threat_strong_ticks (16→12), exhausted_ticks (64→48), idle_min_ticks (32→12), and strong_probability (0.20→0.15). The plan.md serves as reference documentation (line 85: "Tabulky níže slouží jako reference defaultů; reálné runtime hodnoty ber z JSONu"), so mismatch between the table and actual JSON is a genuine documentation defect. The claimed impact (1 full second reduction in max idle) is accurate: 64 ticks - 48 ticks = 16 ticks = 1.0 second at 16 Hz.
verdict.fix: Update plan.md lines 91-120 (the JSON block) to match balance.json. Specifically for idle_max_ticks: change line 105 from "idle_max_ticks": 64, to "idle_max_ticks": 48, and update surrounding enemy_basic values: threat_strong_ticks 16→12, exhausted_ticks 64→48, idle_min_ticks 32→12, strong_probability 0.20→0.15 to match the actual balance.json values at C:\Users\duran\Documents\godot\glamdring\balance.json lines 10-17.

findings.issue: strong_probability documented as 0.20, but balance.json (actual) has 0.15. This changes enemy strong attack frequency from 20% to 15%.

verdict.reasoning: After reading the actual code at C:\Users\duran\Documents\godot\glamdring, this is a genuine defect. plan.md lines 102-128 document `strong_probability` as 0.20 (20% strong attacks), but balance.json line 15 specifies the actual runtime value as 0.15 (15% strong attacks). The code in enemy_basic.gd line 9 correctly reads from Balance.strong_probability, which is loaded from balance.json. This means the actual gameplay has 15% strong enemy attacks, not the 20% documented in plan.md. This is a significant mismatch because it affects enemy threat distribution and difficulty tuning. The documentation should match the actual balance.json value to avoid confusion during future balance iterations and playtesting.
verdict.fix: Update plan.md line 106 from `"strong_probability": 0.20,` to `"strong_probability": 0.15,`. Also update line 128 from `strong_probability — pravděpodobnost strong útoku (0.20 = 20 %)` to `strong_probability — pravděpodobnost strong útoku (0.15 = 15 %)`.

findings.issue: threat_strong documented as 32 ticks, but balance.json has 12. Table row contradicts actual game timing by 20 ticks.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md line 27 to: "| `threat_strong` | 12 | Meč napřažen pro silný útok (vizuálně: enemy obarven červeně) |"

verdict.reasoning: The claim is verified: gameplay.md line 27 documents `threat_strong` as 32 ticks, but balance.json line 11 has `threat_strong_ticks: 12`. This is a genuine 20-tick contradiction in the enemy state timing table. The documented timing is plainly wrong and would mislead anyone reading gameplay.md about how long the enemy's strong attack telegraph actually lasts. Additionally, line 26 has an identical problem with `threat_weak` (documented as 24 ticks in gameplay.md, but balance.json shows 8 ticks). Both threat state durations in the Enemy Stats table are outdated relative to the actual game implementation.
verdict.fix:
Update gameplay.md line 26-27 to: 
| `threat_weak` | 8 | Meč napřažen pro slabý útok |
| `threat_strong` | 12 | Meč napřažen pro silný útok (vizuálně: enemy obarven červeně) |

findings.issue: exhausted documented as 64 ticks, but balance.json has 48. This is a 1-second timing error.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md line 30 to: "| `exhausted` | 48 | `fatigue == 0`; neútočí, neblokuje, všechny útoky zasáhnou |"

verdict.reasoning: The documentation at C:\Users\duran\Documents\godot\glamdring\gameplay.md line 30 lists exhausted as 64 ticks. However, balance.json (line 13) sets "exhausted_ticks": 48, and this value is authoritative — it's loaded at runtime by balance.gd (line 107) and used by enemy.gd's enter_exhausted() method via Balance.exhausted_ticks. The code runs with 48 ticks, not 64. This is a 1-second timing discrepancy (64/16 = 4s documented vs 48/16 = 3s actual). The documentation must match the authoritative runtime value.
verdict.fix:
Update gameplay.md line 30 from:
| `exhausted` | 64 | `fatigue == 0`; neútočí, neblokuje, všechny útoky zasáhnou |

To:
| `exhausted` | 48 | `fatigue == 0`; neútočí, neblokuje, všechny útoky zasáhnou |

findings.issue: idle documented as 32–64 (random), but balance.json has 12–48. Minimum idle is off by 20 ticks (1.25 seconds), maximum by 16 ticks.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md line 25 to: "| `idle` | 12–48 (random) | Čeká mezi útoky, blokuje útoky hráče |"

verdict.reasoning: REAL DEFECT. The documentation in gameplay.md line 25 claims enemy idle is "32–64 (random)" but the authoritative balance.json (line 14) specifies "idle_min_ticks": 12 and "idle_max_ticks": 48. The code in enemy_basic.gd line 13 explicitly uses `Balance.idle_min_ticks` and `Balance.idle_max_ticks`, which are loaded from balance.json at runtime. This creates a significant documentation-code mismatch: the minimum idle duration is actually 12 ticks (off by 20 from the documented 32) and maximum is 48 ticks (off by 16 from the documented 64). This is a major issue because players/designers reading the gameplay spec would be misled about enemy idle timing by 1.25 seconds minimum and 1 second maximum (at 16 ticks/sec).
verdict.fix:
Update gameplay.md line 25 from:
| `idle` | 32–64 (random) | Čeká mezi útoky, blokuje útoky hráče |

to:
| `idle` | 12–48 (random) | Čeká mezi útoky, blokuje útoky hráče |

findings.issue: FSM diagram shows `threat_weak (24t)` and `threat_strong (32t)`, but balance.json has 8 and 12 respectively. These are hardcoded values in the text description that don't match the actual tunable values.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md lines 37-39 to use actual values: threat_weak (8t) and threat_strong (12t). Or generalize with 'N ticks' notation to reference balance.json.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim is correct. gameplay.md contains hardcoded threat duration values that do not match balance.json:
- Line 26 states threat_weak duration as 24 ticks, but balance.json defines it as 8 ticks
- Line 27 states threat_strong duration as 32 ticks, but balance.json defines it as 12 ticks
- Lines 37-39 in the FSM diagram also show these incorrect values (24t and 32t)

This creates a significant documentation-to-code mismatch. Since balance.json is the source of truth (it's a tunables file loaded at runtime), the markdown documentation is misleading developers and players about actual game state durations. This is a major severity issue because it directly affects gameplay understanding and balance communication.
verdict.fix:
Update gameplay.md to match balance.json:
1. Line 26: Change "| 24 |" to "| 8 |" in the threat_weak row
2. Line 27: Change "| 32 |" to "| 12 |" in the threat_strong row
3. Line 37: Change "threat_weak  (24t)" to "threat_weak  (8t)"
4. Line 39: Change "threat_strong (32t)" to "threat_strong (12t)"

Alternatively, as suggested, generalize the documentation to reference balance.json values with notation like "threat_weak (N ticks per balance.json)" to avoid future desynchronization.

findings.issue: FSM shows `exhausted (64t)` but balance.json has 48. The parenthetical tick count should match the balance.json value.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md line 41 to: "fatigue → 0 (kdykoli) ──► exhausted (48t) ──► idle (fatigue := 40)"

verdict.reasoning: The gameplay.md file at line 41 documents the Enemy FSM as "exhausted (64t)" but balance.json line 13 defines "exhausted_ticks": 48. This is a direct factual mismatch between documentation and the canonical balance values. The documentation is used by developers and reviewers to understand game mechanics, so incorrect tick counts constitute a genuine defect. Additionally, the consolidated FSM diagram at line 185 repeats the same error. Both occurrences should be corrected to match the actual balance.json value of 48 ticks.
verdict.fix: Update line 41 and line 185 in gameplay.md: change "exhausted (64t)" to "exhausted (48t)". Line 41 becomes: "fatigue → 0 (kdykoli) ──► exhausted (48t) ──► idle (fatigue := 40)". Line 185 becomes: "fatigue → 0 ───► exhausted (48t) ──► idle (fatigue=40)"

findings.issue: Implied in attack decision that strong is 20% ("80 % weak"), but balance.json has 0.15 (15% strong, 85% weak). This affects AI behavior prediction.
findings.suggestedFix: Update gameplay.md to clarify actual odds. Current phrasing "80 % weak / 20 % strong" conflicts with balance.json 0.15 value. Either fix the doc or the JSON.

verdict.reasoning: The gameplay.md file at lines 37, 39, 180-182, and 202 states the enemy chooses weak/strong attacks at 80%/20% odds. However, balance.json line 15 defines "strong_probability": 0.15 (15%), and balance.gd/enemy_basic.gd load and use this value. The actual game behavior is 85% weak / 15% strong, not 80% weak / 20% strong as documented. This is a genuine defect affecting AI behavior prediction.
verdict.fix: Update gameplay.md lines 37, 39, 180-182, and 202 to reflect 85% weak and 15% strong attacks, matching the balance.json strong_probability value of 0.15. Alternatively, if the design intent is 20% strong, update balance.json to "strong_probability": 0.20 instead.

findings.issue: Base class hardcodes `_stagger_ticks()` to return 12, but balance.json and enemy_basic.gd use Balance.stagger_ticks (16). Subclass overrides correctly, but the base default contradicts the canonical value by 4 ticks.
findings.suggestedFix: Update enemy.gd line 238 to return 16 (or reference a constant) to align with the canonical balance.json value of 16.

verdict.reasoning: The base class Enemy._stagger_ticks() at C:\Users\duran\Documents\godot\glamdring\enemy.gd line 238 returns hardcoded value 12. The canonical balance.json specifies stagger_ticks: 16 (line 16), and Balance.gd correctly exposes this as var stagger_ticks: int = 16 (line 39). The subclass enemy_basic.gd correctly overrides and returns Balance.stagger_ticks (line 36-37), masking the defect. However, any future enemy subclass that doesn't override _stagger_ticks() will use the incorrect base value of 12 instead of the intended 16, creating a 4-tick disparity from the canonical balance parameter.
verdict.fix: Change enemy.gd line 238 from `return 12` to `return Balance.stagger_ticks` to align with the canonical balance value and prevent future subclasses from inheriting an incorrect default.

findings.location: line 123, tunable accessors table / enemy_basic section
findings.issue: Docs claim threat_weak is 8, threat_strong is 16, but the JSON example block (lines 102-107) contradicts itself by showing threat_strong_ticks as 16 in the heading-text and 12 in the JSON. The JSON block is authoritative; the tunable table should match.
findings.suggestedFix: Update plan.md line 124 (threat_weak_ticks / threat_strong_ticks row) to: `threat_weak_ticks` / `threat_strong_ticks` — délka napřahu weak / strong (ticky) — 8 / 12.

verdict.reasoning: The claim correctly identifies a real defect: plan.md's JSON example block (lines 101-108) shows `"threat_strong_ticks": 16` on line 102, but the authoritative balance.json file (line 11) has `"threat_strong_ticks": 12`. This is a factual discrepancy between documentation and source of truth. However, the suggested fix location is incorrect. The defect is in the JSON example block itself (line 102 of plan.md), not in the table description on line 124. Line 124 only describes what the fields mean without claiming specific values, so it doesn't need fixing. The actual fix is to update line 102 in plan.md from `"threat_strong_ticks": 16,` to `"threat_strong_ticks": 12,` to match balance.json.
verdict.fix: Update plan.md line 102 from `"threat_strong_ticks": 16,` to `"threat_strong_ticks": 12,` to match the authoritative values in balance.json line 11. The line should read: `"threat_weak_ticks": 8, "threat_strong_ticks": 12,`

findings.issue: balance.gd hardcodes defaults that differ from balance.json: threat_weak_ticks defaults to 6 (JSON 8), idle_min_ticks defaults to 16 (JSON 12), idle_max_ticks defaults to 48 (correct), strong_probability defaults to 0.15 (correct). These fallback defaults should match balance.json for clarity.
findings.suggestedFix: Update balance.gd defaults: line 31 threat_weak_ticks to 8, line 36 idle_min_ticks to 12. These are cosmetic (JSON is authoritative) but prevent confusion.

verdict.reasoning: After reading balance.gd (lines 31-41) and balance.json (lines 10-17), two fallback defaults do not match the JSON values: threat_weak_ticks is hardcoded as 6 but JSON specifies 8, and idle_min_ticks is hardcoded as 16 but JSON specifies 12. While JSON is authoritative in normal operation (the _load_balance function always loads from JSON if available), these mismatched defaults create a defect: if balance.json is ever missing or malformed, gameplay would silently use incorrect values (enemy weak threats would last 2 ticks shorter, enemy idle would minimum 4 ticks longer). The code structure treats defaults as graceful fallback, and they should therefore match JSON for consistency and to prevent silent behavioral changes if the file becomes unavailable.
verdict.fix: Update balance.gd line 31 from `var threat_weak_ticks: int = 6` to `var threat_weak_ticks: int = 8`, and line 36 from `var idle_min_ticks: int = 16` to `var idle_min_ticks: int = 12`. These changes align the fallback defaults with the authoritative values in balance.json.
